title: Token
description: The smallest unit of text a language model reads and predicts—usually a word piece, not always a whole word. Each token ID maps to a dense vector through vector embedding of model hidden size before attention runs.

whatItIs.title: What It Is
whatItIs.body: A token is one step in the model's discrete text vocabulary. Subword tokenizers often split rare words into pieces so the vocabulary stays finite while still covering open vocabulary text.

whyItMatters.title: Why It Matters
whyItMatters.body: Tokenization sets context length in tokens, shapes cost per request, and determines whether two strings look identical to the model. Mismatched tokenizers between training and serving are a common integration bug. When someone says "128k context," they mean 128k tokens—the same units the tokenizer emits before embeddings and next-token scoring. Use the related docs below to step from tokens into embeddings and onward toward logits and softmax.

simpleExample.title: Simple Example
simpleExample.body: The phrase "language models" might become two or three tokens depending on the tokenizer. Each ID is looked up in an embedding table before attention layers run.

whereItAppears.title: Where It Appears
whereItAppears.body: Tokens appear at the input embedding layer, in autoregressive next-token scoring, and in KV-cache accounting during decoding. Attention modules operate on token positions, not raw characters. From here, follow the curated link to embeddings to continue the token-to-probability chain.

commonConfusions.title: Common Confusions
commonConfusions.body: Tokens are not always words; byte-level BPE can emit single-character tokens. Token count also differs from word count and from Unicode grapheme count.

module.byte-level-tokenization.reason: Byte-level tokenization shows why tokenizer output does not have to line up with whole words.

concept.special-tokens.reason: Special tokens show which reserved markers the tokenizer keeps aside for boundaries, padding, and control signals.

concept.tokenizers-overview.reason: Use the broader tokenizer-family page when you want the preprocessing step, nearby algorithms, and context-cost tradeoffs rather than just the definition of one token.

concept.embedding.reason: Each token ID becomes a learned numerical representation before the model mixes context.

concept.vocabulary-size.reason: That vocabulary count tells you how many ordinary and reserved tokens the tokenizer can emit IDs for.

concept.logit.reason: Next-token prediction starts as a candidate score for each vocabulary token.

concept.softmax.reason: Those candidate scores convert into probabilities across the vocabulary.

conceptMap.alt: Diagram showing text flowing through a tokenizer into token IDs and embeddings
conceptMap.caption: How raw text becomes token IDs before the transformer stack

openingSummary: Models use a fixed tokenizer vocabulary—mapping text to integer IDs that are embedded and scored—to drive next-token prediction rather than reading raw characters directly.
